ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 covers omelette as a general food product; no Burmese-specific fried egg omelette entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried hen egg as the closest defensible category; no specific omelette sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 matrix browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 covers eggs as a food substance; no specific omelette or fried egg omelette concept exists at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one fried egg omelette made from two medium eggs, weighing roughly 100 g after cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173424)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for whole fried egg (FDC ID 173424) used as the primary nutrient basis. Values represent eggs fried in oil, which closely matches a plain Burmese fried egg omelette. Minor adaptation: the Burmese preparation may use slightly more oil; sodium may be marginally higher due to added salt. Values are reported per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs are a negligible-carbohydrate food (0.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature consistently classify eggs and egg-based dishes with negligible carbohydrate as having an effectively zero glycemic response. Per Veritas v2 rules for zero-GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 due to negligible carbohydrate. This is consistent with Atkinson et al. (2021) classification of protein- and fat-dominant foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~207 mg per 100 g) and potential for additional salt in Burmese preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced salt when ordering, as the sodium content of a fried egg omelette can contribute meaningfully to the daily sodium limit of 2000 mg recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (0.8 g per 100 g) and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: A plain fried egg omelette is a low-glycemic food with negligible carbohydrate, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared with minimal oil and salt.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol content (~401 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~3.9 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg yolk consumption due to the high dietary cholesterol and saturated fat content; current evidence supports moderate egg intake (up to one egg per day) as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern, but cooking oil choice and portion frequency should be discussed with a clinician.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A fried egg omelette is a simple, protein-rich egg dish prepared by beating one or more whole eggs and pan-frying them in oil until set, producing a flat or slightly folded cooked egg cake. In Burmese cuisine, the fried egg omelette (known as Kyar Gyee Hmone) is a common everyday dish served alongside rice, noodles, or as part of a multi-dish Burmese meal. The texture is firm on the outside with a slightly tender interior, and the flavour is savoury with a mild eggy richness. It is typically seasoned with salt and sometimes fish sauce or onion in Burmese preparations. Nutritionally, the fried egg omelette is high in protein (approximately 13.6 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (14.8 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrate (0.8 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic food. It is a good source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, vitamin A, selenium, and phosphorus. The high cholesterol content (~401 mg per 100 g) is clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk management. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the negligible carbohydrate content makes this a suitable protein source. Sodium content should be monitored by those managing hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is served at Burmese restaurants and is also a common home-cooked preparation across multiple ethnic communities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a fried egg omelette?
Is fried egg omelette good for diabetics?
Can I eat fried egg omelette if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy for fried whole egg (FDC ID 173424), which is a well-validated approved source. The match is adapted rather than exact because the Burmese fried egg omelette preparation may involve slightly different oil quantities and seasoning. GI is estimated by analogy as eggs are a negligible-carbohydrate food with no direct GI measurement needed. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173424 (Egg, whole, cooked, fried). This is the closest approved-source match for a plain fried egg omelette. 2. Glycemic index is set to 1 (effectively zero) because carbohydrate content is 0.8 g per 100 g, which is below the threshold for meaningful glycemic response. gi_category is 'low' per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 confirmed zero due to negligible carbohydrate. This aligns with Veritas v2 rules for zero-GI foods. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source for fried egg; naturally zero in egg lipids absent industrial hydrogenation. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as eggs contain no dietary fibre. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as eggs contain no vitamin C. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (5.8) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.8) are reported by USDA. 7. The cuisine_tags in the input (Noodle, Lor Mee) appear to be erroneous tags from the field survey platform context and do not describe this dish; they have been disregarded. 8. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' from the input is preserved as it is appropriate for an egg dish. 9. Burmese romanized name 'Kyar Gyee Hmone' is a common romanization of the Burmese term for fried egg omelette; native Burmese script not included in local_names as Burmese is not a mandatory language for this cuisine category under Veritas v2 rules. 10. All aliases from aliases_by_language are present verbatim in the flat aliases list, ensuring consistency as per Veritas v2 rules.
